title: Preliminary Report on Foreign Holdings of U.S. Securities at End-June 2025
summary: The Treasury released preliminary data from the June 2025 annual TIC survey showing total foreign holdings of U.S. securities at $35.33 trillion, up 14.4% from $30.88 trillion in June 2024. Foreign official holdings increased to $6.90 trillion. This is a lagging reference data release (8-month lag) informing macro understanding of foreign capital allocation into U.S. assets.
key_points: Total foreign holdings of U.S. securities as of June 30, 2025: $35,334 billion ($35.33 trillion), up from $30,881 billion year-over-year (+$4,453 billion, +14.4%)
Equity holdings: $19,843 billion (56% of total), up from $16,878 billion (+$2,965 billion, +17.6%)
Long-term debt holdings: $13,839 billion (39% of total), up from $12,688 billion (+$1,151 billion, +9.1%)
Short-term debt holdings: $1,652 billion, up from $1,314 billion (+$338 billion, +25.7%)
Foreign official holdings: $6,904 billion (19.5% of total), up from $6,598 billion (+$306 billion)
Survey conducted jointly by Treasury, Federal Reserve Bank of New York, and Fed Board of Governors
why_it_matters: This data quantifies the scale and composition of foreign ownership of U.S. assets, which informs understanding of potential selling pressure, demand dynamics for Treasuries, and dollar funding markets. The 14.4% annual increase in foreign holdings suggests continued strong global demand for U.S. securities, supporting dollar hegemony and Treasury market demand. Foreign official holdings approaching $7 trillion represent a significant block of non-market-driven holders that can affect price dynamics.
macro_context: This TIC data is a reference dataset for macro analysis, not a catalyst. It provides annual snapshots of foreign portfolio investment patterns. The data shows strong foreign demand for U.S. assets continuing through mid-2025, with notable increases in both equity and debt holdings. Foreign official holdings grew more slowly than private holdings, suggesting private sector drove the increase. This data informs balance of payments analysis, dollar demand assessment, and Treasury market depth analysis.
important_numbers: Top holders: UK ($3,461B), Cayman Islands ($3,177B), Canada ($2,898B), Japan ($2,883B)

All important_numbers values match source data correctly
Top holders list (UK, Cayman, Canada, Japan) matches Table B in source
